back: Two graphs G1 and G2 are isomorphic if there exists a one-to-one correspondence between their vertices that preserves adjacency. The mapping is called an isomorphism.
A subgraph is a graph whose vertex set is a subset of the original graph's vertex set, and whose edge set is a subset of the original graph's edge set.
A walk is a finite sequence of alternating vertices and edges, starting and ending at vertices, where each edge connects consecutive vertices.

front: What is a Path in Graph Theory?
back: A path is a walk in which all vertices are distinct (no vertex is repeated except possibly the starting vertex if it's also the ending vertex).
A circuit is a closed walk that starts and ends at the same vertex, with at least one edge, and does not repeat edges.
A cycle is a circuit where only the first and last vertices are the same, with no other repetitions. A cycle of length k is denoted as Ck.
A spanning subgraph contains all vertices of the original graph but may contain only some edges. It must include every vertex of G.
For a subset S of vertices, the induced subgraph contains exactly those vertices and all edges between pairs of vertices in S from the original graph.
A trail is a walk in which no edge is repeated. All trails are walks, but not all walks are trails.

front: What is the Length of a Walk?
back: The length of a walk is equal to the number of edges in it. A walk of length k contains k edges.
A graph is connected if there exists a path between every pair of vertices. Otherwise, it is disconnected.
A Hamiltonian path visits each vertex exactly once. A Hamiltonian circuit (or Hamiltonian cycle) visits each vertex exactly once and returns to the starting vertex.
An Eulerian trail (or Eulerian path) uses each edge of the graph exactly once. An Eulerian circuit starts and ends at the same vertex.
An automorphism is an isomorphism from a graph to itself. It is a permutation of vertices that preserves adjacency.

front: Define Walk, Trail, Path, Cycle in ascending order of constraints
back: Walk (least restrictive) → Trail (no edge repeat) → Path (no vertex repeat) → Cycle (path + returns to start)
